case_name: Pilot-scale potable reuse treatment for pesticide and uranium control at a remote surface-water site
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for pilot-scale polishing of contaminated surface water intended for potable reuse, balancing pesticide and uranium removal against capital limits, remote operation, and residuals handling constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A remote utilities district is piloting potable reuse using a small surface-water reservoir that receives episodic agricultural runoff and some geogenic uranium contribution from the catchment. The district must show reliable reduction of two pesticides and uranium, but only pilot-scale deployment is currently funded and there is no full-time specialist operator on site. The trade-off is between treatment trains that can address all contaminants with strong compliance confidence and simpler, lower-capital options that are easier to run in a remote location. Residuals hauling from the site is possible but expensive, so options that create difficult brine or hazardous sludge streams are less attractive unless they offer clear performance advantages.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for potable reuse demonstration with robust reduction of mevinphos and tebuconazole to very low levels and uranium below drinking-water-relevant limits.
secondary_goals: keep pilot system simple enough for remote operator oversight
minimize high-strength concentrate or difficult sludge generation
demonstrate treatment robustness during runoff-driven water quality swings
control taste, odor, and organic fouling risk where practical

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer residual streams that can be changed out or hauled periodically; avoid continuous brine production or chemically unstable sludge where possible
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate seasonal turbidity and organic matter variation with limited onsite troubleshooting

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter to stabilize downstream adsorption and ion exchange performance
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: remove carryover solids and protect polishing units
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic pesticides including tebuconazole and provide some mevinphos reduction plus organic polishing
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange_for_uranium: selectively remove dissolved uranium to below potable limits
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse demonstration

applicability_rationale: this train separates the main treatment challenge into a practical organics path and a practical uranium path, avoiding a single high-complexity unit for everything
conventional clarification and filtration are well suited to surface water with moderate turbidity and DOC and improve reliability of downstream media processes
GAC is a strong fit for tebuconazole and other hydrophobic pesticides and adds beneficial polishing for reuse applications
anion exchange is an established route for uranium in oxidized waters and can be deployed at pilot scale with skid-mounted vessels
compared with membrane-heavy alternatives, this route has lower energy demand and avoids continuous concentrate management, which is valuable at a remote site
although two polishing media types are needed, the overall operating approach remains manageable for a pilot with periodic media oversight rather than intensive daily control
potential_risks: mevinphos is more polar and may show less stable adsorption on GAC than tebuconazole, so EBCT and monitoring need attention
anion exchange performance for uranium can be affected by competing anions such as sulfate and alkalinity
spent carbon and exhausted resin still require planned offsite handling or replacement
if turbidity spikes are not well controlled upstream, media life can shorten

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and part of the NOM load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish solids and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide flexible adsorption of pesticides during runoff episodes

applicability_rationale: PAC is attractive in a pilot context because dose can be adjusted during episodic pesticide spikes without committing to large fixed GAC vessels
the route remains lower energy than RO and still avoids continuous concentrate generation
anion exchange addresses uranium with a compact skid that fits pilot-only implementation
this option can be cheaper in upfront capital than a full GAC installation, which matters under funding constraints
the ranking is slightly below the GAC-based train because PAC handling, slurry preparation, and solids capture introduce more routine operational tasks at a remote site
potential_risks: PAC dosing and contact effectiveness can be variable for mixed pesticides and may require bench confirmation
carbon-laden solids increase sludge management burden relative to fixed-bed GAC
mevinphos removal may be less reliable without optimized contact time and dose
uranium removal still depends on resin management and competing ion conditions

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce fouling load before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide broad rejection of uranium and partial to strong rejection of many pesticide molecules
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration offers a compact pilot platform and can address both uranium and pesticides in one principal barrier
this route is technically attractive when demonstration emphasis is on broad-spectrum removal rather than the lowest operator burden
membrane treatment can provide more direct compliance confidence for uranium than GAC-based trains
the option ranks below media-based systems because concentrate handling, fouling control, and operator attention are substantial disadvantages at a remote site with limited staffing
potential_risks: brine management is a major challenge for remote pilot operation
membrane fouling risk rises with seasonal surface-water turbidity and organics
finished water remineralization and corrosion stability may need active control
pilot success may depend heavily on pretreatment quality and chemical supply reliability

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: lower solids and NOM load before oxidation
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect advanced oxidation equipment and downstream media
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_UV_H2O2: oxidatively degrade susceptible pesticide compounds, especially more oxidation-responsive organophosphate contaminants
core_unit_functions.adsorptive_uranium_media: remove dissolved uranium using selective sorptive media
core_unit_functions.biological_or_GAC_polishing: remove oxidation byproducts and provide final organics polishing

applicability_rationale: this route is technically capable and may be attractive if the project specifically wants to demonstrate destructive treatment for mevinphos rather than only phase transfer
advanced oxidation can be relevant for certain pesticides, especially when trace-organic transformation is a program objective
uranium still requires a separate removal step because oxidation does not solve the metal-contaminant problem
the configuration ranks lower because it is more complex, energy intensive, and less naturally aligned with remote pilot constraints than adsorption plus ion exchange
tebuconazole may not be as straightforward to oxidize as more oxidation-responsive compounds, increasing uncertainty
potential_risks: pesticide transformation products may require evaluation rather than assuming complete risk removal
UV and peroxide systems need reliable chemical supply, instrumentation, and operator attention
adsorptive uranium media performance can be sensitive to pH and carbonate chemistry
overall train complexity may be too high for a remote site unless strong technical support is available

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and some NOM
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect GAC and improve effluent clarity
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb pesticides and polish dissolved organics

applicability_rationale: this is an operationally simple and familiar surface-water polishing train for pesticide control
it may perform well for tebuconazole and provide some broader taste and odor benefits
it ranks last because it does not provide a reliable dedicated barrier for uranium, which is a required contaminant in this case
the option is included as a plausible but misaligned route: it fits capital and remote-operation constraints better, yet fails the multi-contaminant treatment objective
potential_risks: uranium removal is likely inadequate for potable reuse compliance needs
mevinphos adsorption may be inconsistent compared with more hydrophobic pesticides
frequent runoff events could accelerate carbon exhaustion
reliance on a train that misses one regulated contaminant creates compliance risk despite operational simplicity

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains combining pesticide-focused adsorption with uranium-specific ion exchange or adsorptive removal should rank near the top
membrane treatment may appear as a strong technical alternative but should not rank first under remote-location and capital constraints
a technically simple pesticide-focused option without a reliable uranium barrier should appear lower despite operational advantages

must_reflect: pilot-only implementation favors modular skid-mounted or conventional-plus-media systems over highly integrated full-scale-style treatment
remote location penalizes options needing intensive monitoring, chemical handling, or difficult troubleshooting
capital and residuals constraints should downgrade RO or NF when brine management becomes a dominant burden

must_contain: why surface-water pretreatment for turbidity and NOM is important before adsorption, ion exchange, or membranes
why uranium generally needs a dedicated removal barrier distinct from pesticide-oriented treatment
why GAC is more favorable for hydrophobic pesticides such as tebuconazole than for more polar compounds such as mevinphos
why membrane and AOP options can be technically capable yet rank lower due to pilot-scale and remote-operation misfit

should_mention: competition from background ions for uranium-selective treatment
DOC and turbidity impacts on media life or membrane fouling
spent media, sludge, or brine management burdens
possible transformation-product concerns for oxidative treatment
